rows.sat_rat: Mandatory-field enforcement and completeness checks in EBRs make missing required evidence uncommon. Assembling mandatory evidence takes time but the failure is missing evidence at decision. Knowing which evidence is mandatory is codified in review checklists, limiting skill dependence. Missing evidence forces re-review cycles that waste QA resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical deviations or out-of-specification investigations unresolved at disposition
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved critical deviations or OOS investigations at disposition is a core completeness failure (relevance 4) with severe release consequence (severity 4), capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Mandatory closure gates for critical/OOS items are standard, making unresolved-at-disposition rare in mature systems. Critical investigations are long-running and their pendency delays disposition, but the need is about them being unresolved. Adjudicating critical deviations and OOS results requires senior investigator expertise. Unresolved critical items force holds that tie up capital and can scrap batches.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the final disposition rationale is unsupported by approved evidence and applicable standards
rows.imp_rat: A final disposition rationale unsupported by approved evidence/standards defeats the purpose of structured review (relevance 4) with serious compliance impact (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Structured disposition templates enforce evidence citation, though completeness of support still varies. Documenting a supported rationale takes time but the failure is inadequate support, not lateness. Constructing a defensible rationale tied to evidence and standards requires strong regulatory writing skill. Unsupported rationales invite costly audit findings and re-review.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required disposition approvals that are missing or invalid
rows.imp_rat: Missing or invalid required disposition approvals is a core gate failure (relevance 4) with serious release-legitimacy consequence (severity 4), held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Mandatory signature workflows reliably prevent missing or invalid approvals in validated EBRs. Collecting approvals adds routing time but the error is their absence or invalidity. Ensuring approval validity is enforced by system rules, not reviewer skill. Missing approvals trigger rework and audit exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that disposition proceeds before all relevant deviations are adjudicated
rows.imp_rat: Disposition proceeding before all deviations are adjudicated is a core review-completeness failure (relevance 4) with serious release consequence (severity 4), held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: EBR/deviation-management integration usually blocks disposition until open deviations close, giving strong control. Waiting for all deviations to close before disposition is a recognized source of delay but the error is premature proceeding. Tracking that every relevant deviation is adjudicated is largely procedural rather than expertise-limited. Proceeding early risks costly rework and regulatory citations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batch records advanced to disposition with release-relevant deviations unresolved
rows.imp_rat: Advancing records to disposition with release-relevant deviations unresolved is a central review-gate failure (relevance 4) with serious safety/compliance stakes (severity 4), at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Linked deviation status flags in EBRs reliably surface open items, though relevance filtering is imperfect. Chasing unresolved deviations before disposition consumes time but the target is the unresolved state itself. Identifying release-relevant deviations still needs qualified reviewer judgement. Advancing records with open deviations wastes downstream QA effort and delays inventory turns.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batch records lacking valid required approval before disposition
rows.imp_rat: Batch records lacking valid required approval before disposition is a core gate failure (relevance 4) with serious compliance consequence (severity 4), held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic workflow enforces required e-signatures before disposition, making invalid approvals rare. Approval routing takes time but the failure is a missing or invalid approval. Verifying valid approval is procedural, requiring little specialized skill. A missing approval invalidates release and forces costly rework.

rows.stk: Data Integrity / Computer System Validation Specialist
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batch-data handoffs lacking validation criteria for content, sequence, and attribution
rows.imp_rat: Batch-data handoffs lacking content/sequence/attribution validation directly threaten faithful capture and structuring of execution data (relevance 4); severity is meaningful since broken handoffs corrupt the review record.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-data handoffs between systems often lack rigorous validation criteria, leaving attribution and sequence gaps. Defining handoff criteria adds analysis time across system boundaries. Specifying content, sequence and attribution at handoffs needs cross-system integration expertise. Weak handoff validation leads to costly data-integrity failures at interfaces.

rows.stk: Data Integrity / Computer System Validation Specialist
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of electronic signature controls omitted from validation criteria for batch review and disposition
rows.imp_rat: Omitting e-signature controls from validation criteria weakens attribution central to capturing reviewable batch data (relevance 4), and severity is moderate since missing signature validation can invalidate disposition, but ranking caps this below batch 1.
rows.sat_rat: E-signature controls are commonly validated but coverage of every review/disposition point in criteria is sometimes incomplete. E-signature validation is defined once during system validation. Part requirements are well documented, reducing reliance on rare expertise. Signature controls are standard EBR features with modest validation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which evaluation criteria permit loss of batch data lineage, source attribution, chronology, or context across system boundaries
rows.imp_rat: Criteria permitting loss of data lineage/attribution/chronology across boundaries undermines the very integrity of captured batch data (relevance 4, severity 4), held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard practices catch gross lineage breaks, but subtle loss of source attribution and context across boundaries remains a common, under-caught failure. Tracing lineage and context loss across boundaries is a slow analytical task that competes with delivery timelines. Detecting subtle context or lineage loss across system handoffs requires specialized data-governance skill that is not universally available. Lineage loss surfaces as costly investigations and data reconciliation later, with little upfront preventive investment today.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batch dispositions with broken evidentiary chains across actions, exceptions, assessments, and approvals
rows.imp_rat: Broken evidentiary chains across actions/exceptions/approvals directly break the structured record needed for review (relevance 4) with serious traceability consequence (severity 4), capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: EBR systems link some records automatically, but evidentiary chains still break across systems and exception handling. Reconstructing evidentiary chains across actions, exceptions, and approvals is time-consuming when links are not native. Tracing chains requires experienced reviewers to mentally connect disparate records. Broken chains cause costly re-investigation and inspection findings.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an actionable deviation indication is overlooked during concurrent run demands
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking an actionable deviation indication under concurrent-run load is a serious miss (severity 4) but concerns operational vigilance more than the capture/structuring job (relevance 3).
rows.sat_rat: Human vigilance under load is unreliable, so this is poorly served without automated alerting. Noticing a deviation indication amid concurrent demands can be delayed. Not overlooking an actionable indication under multitasking is a severe attention/vigilance burden this need targets. Overlooked deviations lead to costly batch impact.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an incorrect approved range is applied to a critical process parameter during the run
rows.imp_rat: Applying an incorrect approved range to a critical parameter during the run risks wrong pass/fail (severity 4); relevance moderate as an in-run control adjacent to review capture (relevance 3).
rows.sat_rat: Pre-specified ranges in the master record make wrong-range application uncommon. Confirming the correct approved range is applied is a quick reference check. Applying the right range depends on operator/config knowledge of the batch spec. A wrong range can pass an out-of-spec batch, an extreme cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical process parameter observations that differ from the actual process condition
rows.imp_rat: Observations differing from actual process conditions corrupt the truth of captured data (severity 4) but this measurement-accuracy issue is somewhat upstream of structuring for review (relevance 3).
rows.sat_rat: Manual observation introduces recurring mismatches versus automated sensing. Confirming an observation matches actual condition adds a check moment. Accurate CPP observation depends on operator reading and instrument skill. Wrong CPP observations can invalidate a batch at high cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the executable batch record differs from current approved master instructions
rows.imp_rat: An executable record differing from current approved master instructions can invalidate the whole batch (severity 4); relevance moderate as it is a control upstream of capture (relevance 3).
rows.sat_rat: Controlled issuance and version control make version mismatches quite rare. Verifying the executable record matches current master takes issuance-time checks. Ensuring the right version is issued relies on QA document-control knowledge. An outdated record in use can invalidate a batch at high cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unexplained in-process or release results incorrectly classified as not requiring resolution
rows.imp_rat: Misclassifying unexplained results as not requiring resolution can hide real problems (severity 4); relevance moderate as a judgment error within evaluation (relevance 3).
rows.sat_rat: Classification of in-process/release results relies on manual judgement, so unexplained results are sometimes wrongly cleared. Reviewing whether flagged results truly need resolution takes reviewer time. Correctly classifying results as needing resolution requires experienced judgement. Misclassified unexplained results risk releasing defects, a severe cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of yield, fill, component, or material reconciliation results inconsistent with source batch data
rows.imp_rat: Reconciliation results inconsistent with source batch data can lead to wrong disposition (severity 4) but reconciliation is one facet of the broader capture job (relevance 3).
rows.sat_rat: Automated reconciliation-to-source checks are limited, so inconsistencies between reconciliation results and source data recur frequently. Comparing reconciliation results to source batch data is a slow manual calculation check. Detecting yield/fill/material inconsistencies demands numerate, experienced reviewers. Inconsistent reconciliation surfaces as costly material-balance investigations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that batch evidence with unverifiable provenance is accepted as valid during adjudication
rows.imp_rat: Accepting evidence with unverifiable provenance during adjudication is a serious integrity failure (severity 4); relevance moderate as an evidence-vetting concern (relevance 3).
rows.sat_rat: Audit trails support provenance for system data, but attachments and manual inputs still enter with weaker provenance verification. Checking provenance adds some review time but audit trails speed it. Judging provenance validity of borderline evidence needs data-integrity expertise. Accepting unverifiable evidence risks costly compliance failures.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that captured batch data become unavailable or unreadable before disposition
rows.imp_rat: Captured data becoming unavailable or unreadable before disposition is a severe integrity/availability failure (severity 4); relevance moderate as a durability concern adjacent to capture (relevance 3).
rows.sat_rat: Validated EBR systems with backup and archival make data becoming unavailable or unreadable before disposition genuinely rare, a best-served need. Electronic archival provides near-instant retrieval, avoiding retrieval delays. Data preservation is handled by validated infrastructure, requiring little user skill. Reliable electronic storage avoids costs of lost or damaged records.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of deviations affecting a batch that are absent from its resolution scope
rows.imp_rat: Deviations affecting a batch absent from its resolution scope risks unmanaged issues (severity 4); relevance moderate as a scoping completeness concern (relevance 3).
rows.sat_rat: Deviations affecting a batch are sometimes absent from its resolution scope because linkage between deviation and batch systems is imperfect. Confirming all affecting deviations are in scope adds review time. Identifying every deviation affecting a batch requires expert cross-referencing. Missed deviations in scope risk defective release and cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required sterility-assurance evidence missing at disposition review
rows.imp_rat: Missing sterility-assurance evidence at disposition review is severe for safe release (severity 4) but is a specific evidence subset within the broader capture job (relevance 3).
rows.sat_rat: Sterility-assurance evidence is well-controlled but its arrival at review is not always confirmed complete, leaving occasional gaps. Lab and sterility results interfaces speed evidence availability when integrated. Tracking which sterility evidence is required needs quality expertise but is routine. Missing sterility evidence causes expensive holds.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of erroneous calculations or transcribed values used for disposition
rows.imp_rat: Erroneous calculations or transcribed values used for disposition can drive wrong release decisions (severity 4) but are a specific accuracy defect within capture (relevance 3).
rows.sat_rat: Validated automated calculations make erroneous calculated values used for disposition fairly rare. Automated calculations remove manual computation time and its rechecking. System-embedded calculations remove reliance on operator arithmetic skill, directly addressing this need. Fewer calculation errors reduce rework and rejection cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical contamination or sterility exception is omitted from evidence considered for disposition
rows.imp_rat: Omitting a critical contamination/sterility exception from disposition evidence is severe for patient safety (severity 4) but is a specific edge case relative to general data capture (relevance 3).
rows.sat_rat: Deviation-linkage and exception flagging in EBRs reduce omissions, but cross-system evidence still gets missed occasionally. Compiling contamination exceptions takes effort but the failure mode is omission, not delay. Recognizing which contamination signals are disposition-critical requires expert sterile-process knowledge. A missed sterility exception can trigger recalls with enormous cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which batch data used for disposition has untraceable provenance or unexplained alteration
rows.imp_rat: Untraceable provenance or unexplained alteration of disposition data is a severe integrity concern (severity 4) but framed as an ambiguity subset (relevance 3).
rows.sat_rat: Electronic audit trails and e-signatures make provenance and alteration highly traceable in EBR systems. Provenance tracing is quick when audit trails exist, so time is minor here. Interpreting audit trails for unexplained alterations needs some data-integrity literacy but tools assist. Untraceable data undermines the whole release and can force costly investigations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that incorrect regulatory release requirements are applied to the batch
rows.imp_rat: Applying incorrect regulatory release requirements risks an invalid release (severity 4); relevance moderate as a criteria-selection issue adjacent to capture (relevance 3).
rows.sat_rat: Requirement mapping is often manually maintained, leaving real risk of misapplication across markets. Determining applicable requirements takes upfront time but is not primarily a delay. Applying correct regulatory release requirements per market demands specialized regulatory expertise. Wrong requirements risk market rejection and expensive rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of underestimating deviation impact on sterility assurance or critical quality attributes before disposition
rows.imp_rat: Underestimating deviation impact on sterility/critical quality attributes is severe for release safety (severity 4) but is a judgment error one step removed from data capture (relevance 3).
rows.sat_rat: Structured impact-assessment templates help consistency, but conclusions still vary with reviewer expertise. Impact assessment adds review cycles but the bottleneck is analytical judgement rather than clock time. Estimating deviation impact on sterility assurance and CQAs is one of the most expert-dependent judgements in the whole job. Underestimation leads to costly field actions and lost batches when the true impact surfaces later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of release-relevant limit excursions not identified before disposition
rows.imp_rat: Release-relevant limit excursions not identified before disposition can lead to unsafe release (severity 4); relevance moderate as detection depends on prior capture (relevance 3).
rows.sat_rat: Automated in-spec/out-of-spec checks against configured limits catch most excursions reliably. Limit checking is fast when automated, so time is secondary. Automated limit flags reduce reliance on reviewer vigilance for excursions. Missed excursions cause expensive downstream failures and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an obsolete or inapplicable release standard is used to assess a batch record
rows.imp_rat: Using an obsolete/inapplicable release standard to assess a record can void the disposition (severity 4); relevance moderate as a criteria-governance issue adjacent to capture (relevance 3).
rows.sat_rat: Version-controlled specification management helps, but linkage of current standards to each batch record is inconsistent. Confirming standard currency is quick but the error is applying an obsolete one. Recognizing the applicable standard version requires regulatory-affairs knowledge. Using an obsolete standard can void a release and force costly reassessment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an unauthorized alteration to captured batch data before disposition
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized alteration of captured batch data is a severe integrity breach (severity 4); relevance moderate as it is a security control adjacent to capture structuring (relevance 3).
rows.sat_rat: Role-based permissions, locked records, and audit trails make unauthorized alteration very unlikely in validated EBRs. Access controls operate in real time so this is not a time concern. Preventing unauthorized alteration relies on configured controls rather than reviewer skill. System access controls are already funded infrastructure, so marginal cost is low.

rows.stk: Data Integrity / Computer System Validation Specialist
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical sterile fill-finish record elements omitted from validation criteria
rows.imp_rat: Omitting critical sterile fill-finish elements from validation criteria has high potential consequence (severity 4), but is a narrower subset relative to the general capture job (relevance 3).
rows.sat_rat: Critical sterile fill-finish elements are sometimes omitted from validation criteria due to specialized domain complexity. Cataloging sterile fill-finish record elements is detailed upfront work. Identifying every critical fill-finish element requires deep aseptic-process and regulatory expertise. Omitting elements risks costly compliance findings and batch loss.

rows.stk: Data Integrity / Computer System Validation Specialist
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of interruption, correction, override, and deviation workflows lacking validation criteria
rows.imp_rat: Missing validation criteria for interruption/correction/override/deviation workflows undermines structured capture of exception data (relevance 4); severity moderate as unvalidated workflows can propagate errors, but bounded by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Correction, override and deviation workflows are frequently under-specified in validation criteria, a persistent weak spot. Elaborating exception-workflow criteria extends validation timelines. Anticipating all interruption, override and deviation paths demands seasoned process knowledge. Missed exception criteria drive expensive deviations and revalidation.

rows.stk: Data Integrity / Computer System Validation Specialist
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency in field definitions, units, statuses, and contextual relationships across batch records
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent field definitions/units/statuses across batch records directly harms structuring of data for review (relevance 4); severity moderate since inconsistencies are often reconcilable.
rows.sat_rat: Field definition, unit and status inconsistency across batch records is common where governance is immature. Harmonizing definitions across records is a slow governance effort. Establishing consistent field definitions and units requires data-governance expertise. Inconsistency causes rework and integration cost over time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required batch execution data exchanges with existing manufacturing and quality systems omitted from the evaluation criteria
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required data exchanges with manufacturing/quality systems from evaluation criteria leaves capture incomplete (relevance 4); severity moderate as gaps may be caught in review.
rows.sat_rat: Data-exchange inventories are commonly incomplete because they depend on tribal knowledge, making omissions a recurring reliability gap. Cataloguing all required data exchanges is a lengthy manual exercise usually truncated under schedule pressure. Knowing every batch data exchange that must occur requires cross-functional expertise spanning production, QC, and IT that few individuals hold. Omitted exchanges create expensive late integration work, and there is no cheap discovery aid in the status quo.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the recommended integration direction compromises attribution, chronology, or audit history for batch execution data
rows.imp_rat: An integration direction that compromises attribution/chronology/audit history is a serious data-integrity risk (severity 4) but is an architectural concern somewhat adjacent to core capture (relevance 3).
rows.sat_rat: Mature regulatory expectations and standard data-integrity practices make attribution and chronology fairly well guarded, though cross-system boundaries still introduce occasional gaps. Audit-trail and attribution checks are established steps but add review time to any integration assessment. Data integrity (ALCOA+) principles are well understood by quality professionals, reducing the skill burden for evaluating attribution and chronology. Preserving audit history is a mandated cost that organizations already budget for, so it is not treated as avoidable waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical deviations not linked to investigation, impact assessment, and disposition evidence
rows.imp_rat: Critical deviations not linked to investigation/impact/disposition evidence risks unsafe release (severity 4); relevance moderate as a linkage completeness concern (relevance 3).
rows.sat_rat: Critical deviations get closer scrutiny so linkage is fairly good, though not fully guaranteed. Assembling investigation, impact, and disposition evidence for critical deviations is time-intensive. Ensuring complete linkage for critical deviations relies on experienced quality staff. Missing linkage for critical deviations risks costly regulatory findings.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of assigning evaluation requirements to the wrong product, jurisdiction, or manufacturing stage
rows.imp_rat: Assigning evaluation requirements to the wrong product/jurisdiction/stage can invalidate the assessment (severity 4); relevance moderate as a criteria-targeting concern (relevance 3).
rows.sat_rat: Assignment is manual and error-prone, so misapplication to the wrong product, jurisdiction, or stage remains a real risk. Verifying correct product/jurisdiction/stage assignment adds checking time. Correct assignment relies on expert knowledge of product and jurisdictional scope. Misassignment causes expensive rework and potential compliance failures.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between a process-condition change and its recognition as a potential deviation
rows.imp_rat: Delay in recognizing a process-condition change as a potential deviation affects timeliness of capture (relevance 3); severity moderate because late recognition can still be corrected before disposition.
rows.sat_rat: Manual monitoring makes timely deviation recognition inconsistent. Recognizing a condition change as a possible deviation is often delayed until manual review of readings. Spotting a nascent deviation demands experienced interpretation of trends. Late recognition widens the excursion window and its remediation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required process-condition observations missing during deviation assessment
rows.imp_rat: Missing process-condition observations during deviation assessment weakens capture completeness (relevance 3); severity moderate as gaps may be identified in review.
rows.sat_rat: Required observations are often missing at assessment time in manual regimes. Gathering all needed observations during assessment takes time. Knowing which observations are required needs assessment expertise. Missing observations force re-sampling or expand investigations, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between the deviation threshold and the approved instruction for the active batch step
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between deviation threshold and approved instruction for the active step risks misclassification (relevance 3); severity moderate and correctable during review.
rows.sat_rat: Static thresholds often don't track step-specific instructions, a recurring mismatch. Reconciling thresholds to the active step's instruction takes setup time. Matching threshold to instruction requires technical configuration knowledge. Mismatched thresholds cause false or missed deviations, both costly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in descriptions of departures from approved execution
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in descriptions of departures from approved execution hinders assessment (relevance 3); severity moderate as unclear descriptions can delay resolution.
rows.sat_rat: Description quality varies widely by author, so ambiguity recurs. Vague departure descriptions cause back-and-forth that delays closure. Describing a departure unambiguously requires writing and technical skill. Ambiguous descriptions inflate investigation effort and cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between observing a critical process parameter and its availability for intervention
rows.imp_rat: Delay between observing a critical parameter and its availability for intervention affects timely capture (relevance 3); severity moderate since it concerns in-run response more than review structuring.
rows.sat_rat: Real-time availability is weak where automated alarms are absent. The gap between observing a critical parameter and it being available to act on is significant with manual logging. Interpreting the observation for intervention needs skilled operators. Delay before intervention prolongs out-of-range exposure, raising cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batch exceptions lacking linked assessment or disposition evidence in the assembled record
rows.imp_rat: Batch exceptions lacking linked assessment/disposition evidence in the assembled record hurt structuring (relevance 3); severity moderate as linkages are recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Cross-system linkage between exceptions and their assessment evidence is weak, so a meaningful share of exceptions lack linked evidence in the assembled record. Compiling linked assessment/disposition evidence for each exception is slow manual assembly. Knowing what evidence each exception requires demands trained reviewers. Missing linked evidence causes costly disposition delays.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of departures from approved instructions lacking documented assessment
rows.imp_rat: Departures from approved instructions lacking documented assessment affect record completeness (relevance 3); severity moderate since assessment can be added.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation capture is not fully automated from execution data, so departures lacking documented assessment still occur regularly. Identifying and assessing every departure from instructions is time-intensive manual work. Judging which departures need assessment requires experienced reviewers. Unassessed departures reaching disposition risk costly regulatory findings.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that applicability of a conditional instruction cannot be determined from captured batch facts
rows.imp_rat: Inability to determine applicability of conditional instructions from captured facts hinders evaluation (relevance 3); severity moderate as it can lead to wrong handling.
rows.sat_rat: Conditional instructions are seldom machine-evaluated against captured facts, so applicability often cannot be cleanly determined. Determining conditional-instruction applicability from captured facts is slow manual reasoning. Interpreting conditional logic against batch facts requires experienced reviewers. Undeterminable applicability drives costly clarification cycles.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that conflicting acceptance criteria cause the same batch result to receive inconsistent exception classifications
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting acceptance criteria causing inconsistent exception classifications introduces instability (relevance 3); severity moderate as inconsistency can be adjudicated.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance criteria often reside in multiple documents without a single authoritative source, so the same result can be classified inconsistently. Reconciling conflicting acceptance criteria consumes review time. Choosing the correct acceptance criteria under conflict demands expert judgement. Inconsistent exception classification risks costly wrong dispositions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of identified exceptions lacking the batch step, material, event sequence, or rationale needed for resolution
rows.imp_rat: Exceptions lacking step/material/sequence/rationale needed for resolution weaken structured capture (relevance 3); severity moderate as missing context can delay but not always corrupt outcomes.
rows.sat_rat: Exceptions frequently arrive without the full contextual detail needed for resolution, as capture tools don't compel it. Gathering step, material, sequence and rationale context for each exception is slow. Assembling resolution context requires experienced investigators piecing together sources. Incomplete exception context forces costly rework loops.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of component-usage and batch-yield discrepancies left unreconciled before disposition
rows.imp_rat: Unreconciled component-usage/yield discrepancies before disposition affect data completeness (relevance 3); severity moderate since reconciliation typically flags before release.
rows.sat_rat: Reconciliation calculations are often semi-manual, leaving component-usage and yield discrepancies unresolved before disposition at a meaningful rate. Manual reconciliation of component usage and yield before disposition is slow. Resolving usage/yield discrepancies requires skilled analysts and material knowledge. Unreconciled discrepancies at disposition force costly holds or rejections.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of false event conflicts caused by inconsistent timestamps or clock references
rows.imp_rat: False event conflicts from inconsistent timestamps cause rework in reviewing sequence (relevance 3); severity moderate as they slow review rather than corrupt disposition outright.
rows.sat_rat: Unsynchronized clocks across integrated systems still generate spurious event conflicts, and time synchronization is inconsistently enforced. Reconciling clock references across systems to dismiss false conflicts is time-consuming. Recognizing false conflicts from clock drift requires attentive, knowledgeable reviewers. False conflicts waste investigation resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recorded exceptions lacking a corresponding deviation reference or documented resolution
rows.imp_rat: Exceptions lacking a deviation reference or documented resolution weaken structured records (relevance 3); severity moderate as linkages can be reconstructed in review.
rows.sat_rat: Exception-to-deviation linkage is frequently manual across systems, so exceptions lacking references or resolution recur. Linking each exception to a deviation reference or resolution is a manual, slow step. Ensuring exceptions carry deviation references depends on reviewer diligence. Exceptions without documented resolution force costly review returns.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that conflicting master-record and review requirements make evidence applicability unclear
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting master-record and review requirements making evidence applicability unclear introduces ambiguity (relevance 3); severity moderate as conflicts are resolvable.
rows.sat_rat: Conflicting requirements across master records and review procedures make evidence applicability genuinely unclear, with no automated arbitration. Reconciling conflicting master-record and review requirements adds review time. Resolving which requirement governs demands senior interpretive skill. Unclear applicability causes costly rework and inconsistent decisions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of ambiguous evidence items lacking the batch context needed for adjudication
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous evidence items lacking needed batch context impedes adjudication (relevance 3); severity moderate as context can often be retrieved.
rows.sat_rat: Ambiguous evidence lacking batch context still requires manual clarification with recurring frequency. Chasing missing batch context for ambiguous items delays adjudication. Supplying missing context relies on experienced reviewers reconstructing it. Context-gathering effort adds labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of adjudication records whose rationale does not connect the evidence to applicable disposition criteria
rows.imp_rat: Adjudication rationale not connecting evidence to applicable criteria weakens defensibility (relevance 3); severity moderate as rationale can be strengthened.
rows.sat_rat: Adjudication rationale frequently fails to explicitly connect evidence to criteria because this is largely manual and unstandardized. Writing rationale that connects evidence to criteria is time-consuming. Articulating a sound evidence-to-criteria rationale is a demanding expert writing task that varies widely by reviewer. Weak rationale causes audit findings and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of changes to captured batch data lacking complete attribution or rationale
rows.imp_rat: Changes lacking complete attribution or rationale is a data-integrity gap (relevance 3); severity moderate as it weakens defensibility of the record.
rows.sat_rat: Audit trails automatically capture attribution and require change rationale, making unattributed changes rare, a well-served need. Automatic attribution capture saves time otherwise spent documenting changes. System-enforced change reasons reduce reliance on user discipline. Complete attribution avoids costly integrity investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of captured event timestamps inconsistent with the documented execution sequence
rows.imp_rat: Timestamps inconsistent with documented sequence create sequence ambiguity (relevance 3); severity moderate and often reconcilable during review.
rows.sat_rat: System-generated timestamps are largely consistent, but manual entries and multi-system merges still create occasional inconsistencies. Consistent timestamps avoid time spent reconciling sequence disputes. Detecting inconsistent timestamps needs a careful reviewer but tools flag anomalies. Timestamp inconsistencies drive investigation cost when found.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of captured batch values violating defined ranges, units, or formats
rows.imp_rat: Captured values violating defined ranges/units/formats degrade data quality (relevance 3); severity moderate as validation typically flags these for correction.
rows.sat_rat: Configured edit checks in mature EBRs make range/unit/format violations uncommon, a well-served need. Real-time format/range validation prevents downstream time spent finding bad values. Automated range and format checks reduce reliance on operator precision. Catching out-of-range values early avoids costly late rejections.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of data integrity exceptions remaining unresolved when disposition is due
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved data-integrity exceptions when disposition is due affects readiness (relevance 3); severity moderate as they can be flagged before final release.
rows.sat_rat: Data-integrity exceptions remaining open at disposition is a recurring pain because resolution depends on slow cross-functional investigation. Unresolved data-integrity exceptions extend disposition timelines. Resolving data-integrity exceptions demands specialized investigation expertise. Open integrity exceptions at disposition carry major compliance cost risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that recorded event chronology fails to establish the actual batch execution order
rows.imp_rat: Recorded chronology failing to establish actual execution order undermines sequence structuring (relevance 3); severity moderate as order can sometimes be reconstructed.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic timestamping usually establishes true execution order, though clock/interface issues occasionally break it. System timestamps establish order automatically, saving reconstruction time. Automated chronology reduces reliance on reviewers reconstructing sequence. Fewer chronology disputes reduce investigation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of execution records not linked to the applicable batch, material, equipment, or operation
rows.imp_rat: Execution records not linked to batch/material/equipment/operation break structuring context (relevance 3); severity moderate as linkages can often be re-established.
rows.sat_rat: EBR context binding reliably links most execution records, with residual gaps at external-system boundaries. Automatic linkage saves manual cross-referencing time. System-enforced associations reduce dependence on user care to link records. Fewer unlinked records reduce investigation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable regulatory or procedural requirements omitted from disposition review
rows.imp_rat: Omitting applicable regulatory/procedural requirements from disposition review affects compliance completeness (relevance 3); severity moderate and often detectable.
rows.sat_rat: Review checklists cover common requirements, but coverage completeness still depends on reviewer knowledge, leaving gaps. Checklist-driven review manages requirement coverage timing. Knowing all applicable regulatory/procedural requirements demands broad regulatory expertise. Omitted requirements risk regulatory findings and costly remediation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of deviations closed with undetermined impact on the batch
rows.imp_rat: Deviations closed with undetermined batch impact leave ambiguity (relevance 3); severity moderate as impact can be revisited but risks premature closure.
rows.sat_rat: Deviations closed with undetermined impact still occur because impact assessment quality varies across investigators. Impact determination adds time but is bounded by workflow. Assessing batch impact of a deviation is a high-skill quality judgement. Undetermined-impact closures create downstream risk and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of deviations closed without complete approved resolution evidence
rows.imp_rat: Deviations closed without complete approved resolution evidence weaken record completeness (relevance 3); severity moderate as gaps may surface on audit.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation systems enforce some evidence attachment, but closures with incomplete approved evidence still happen. Verifying complete resolution evidence lengthens closure review slightly. Confirming resolution evidence completeness relies on reviewer diligence. Incomplete resolution evidence causes audit exposure and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of deviations with conflicting resolution status across batch and deviation records
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting deviation resolution status across records is instability affecting reliability (relevance 3); severity moderate as conflicts can be reconciled.
rows.sat_rat: Because EBR and deviation systems are often separate, conflicting resolution status remains a recurring reliability gap. Reconciling status across systems adds time when they diverge. Spotting conflicting deviation status across records needs attentive cross-checking skill. Status conflicts drive rework and holds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time that disposition waits for required sterility-assurance and laboratory results after execution review
rows.imp_rat: Disposition waiting for sterility/lab results is a delay affecting throughput (relevance 3); severity moderate as it slows rather than compromises the decision.
rows.sat_rat: Results reliably arrive; the chronic issue is the long wait, which the status quo barely mitigates. Sterility and lab result turnaround is a dominant, largely irreducible wait that stalls disposition. The delay is driven by test incubation and lab throughput, not reviewer skill. Extended holds waiting on lab results carry significant inventory-carrying cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the applicable release or rejection criteria for the batch
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in release/rejection criteria affects evaluation clarity (relevance 3); severity moderate as criteria can be clarified before the decision.
rows.sat_rat: Specifications are generally well-defined, but conditional and edge-case criteria retain ambiguity that reviewers must resolve manually. Clear criteria configured in the system speed the decision. Interpreting ambiguous release criteria still demands experienced QA judgement. Ambiguity-driven rework or wrong decisions carry cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from batch execution completion until all required disposition evidence is available
rows.imp_rat: Delay until all disposition evidence is available affects throughput (relevance 3); severity moderate as it delays rather than corrupts the review.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence eventually becomes available reliably; the problem is duration rather than certainty. Waiting for all disposition evidence to become available after execution is a primary source of cycle-time delay. Coordinating timely evidence availability requires operational skill but not deep expertise. Idle batches awaiting evidence tie up capital and capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable conditional disposition requirements omitted from the decision
rows.imp_rat: Omitting applicable conditional disposition requirements from the decision affects completeness (relevance 3); severity moderate as omissions may surface on audit.
rows.sat_rat: Conditional requirements are inconsistently captured because they are often not systematized, so omissions recur. Identifying all conditional requirements adds review time. Recognizing which conditional disposition requirements apply is expert-dependent and easily missed. Omitted conditional requirements cause rework and compliance exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts among batch entries, source records, and approved results at disposition
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflicts among entries/source records/approved results at disposition undermines reliability (relevance 3); severity moderate as conflicts can be reconciled.
rows.sat_rat: Manual cross-referencing dominates, so conflicts among heterogeneous records are inconsistently caught. Resolving conflicts among sources takes time but the error is unresolved conflict at disposition. Reconciling discrepancies among entries, source records, and results requires careful expert cross-checking. Unresolved conflicts force holds and rework, consuming resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batches entering review without all required release evidence available
rows.imp_rat: Batches entering review without all release evidence is a readiness delay (relevance 3); severity moderate as it stalls rather than corrupts review.
rows.sat_rat: Readiness checks exist but records still enter review with gaps fairly often. Waiting for all evidence before review starts is a real delay but the error is incompleteness at entry. Confirming evidence readiness is largely a checklist task. Starting review incomplete wastes reviewer time on stop-start cycles.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of deviation adjudications based on incomplete supporting evidence
rows.imp_rat: Deviation adjudications based on incomplete evidence risk wrong conclusions (relevance 3); severity moderate as incomplete basis can be caught in review.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation systems prompt for evidence but do not guarantee completeness, leaving recurring gaps. Gathering supporting evidence for adjudication takes time but incompleteness is the failure. Judging evidence sufficiency for a deviation requires investigator expertise. Incomplete adjudications get reopened, wasting investigation resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that changed batch evidence is not reassessed after deviation adjudication
rows.imp_rat: Not reassessing changed evidence after adjudication risks stale conclusions (relevance 3); severity moderate as re-review can catch this before release.
rows.sat_rat: Few systems automatically trigger re-review when adjudication changes evidence, so this is inconsistently caught. Reassessment after adjudication adds a loop but the failure is skipping it. Recognizing what changed evidence must be reassessed needs experienced judgement. Skipped reassessment can allow flawed release, costing recalls.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of deviation adjudications with rationale unsupported by the release decision evidence
rows.imp_rat: Adjudications with rationale unsupported by release-decision evidence weaken defensibility (relevance 3); severity moderate as gaps can be addressed before finalizing.
rows.sat_rat: Templates encourage evidence linkage but rationale quality still varies by reviewer. Aligning rationale to evidence adds documentation time, not the core issue. Writing rationale genuinely supported by evidence demands analytical and regulatory skill. Unsupported rationales cause costly re-review and findings.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting values between structured batch data and supporting evidence
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting values between structured data and supporting evidence creates ambiguity (relevance 3); severity moderate as conflicts can be reconciled in review.
rows.sat_rat: EBRs reduce transcription conflicts via structured capture, but supporting-evidence mismatches persist. Comparing structured data to evidence is time-consuming but not the primary loss. Spotting value conflicts needs attentive review though tools can assist. Conflicting values trigger reconciliation effort and delay.

rows.stk: Data Integrity / Computer System Validation Specialist
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in compliance criteria for batch execution data
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in compliance criteria for batch data degrades evaluation clarity (relevance 3); severity moderate as ambiguity can be resolved through review.
rows.sat_rat: Compliance criteria for batch data frequently retain interpretive ambiguity, a recurring source of inconsistent outcomes. Clarifying compliance criteria is deliberative, not time-critical, work. Removing ambiguity in compliance criteria depends on scarce regulatory interpretation skill. Ambiguous criteria cause costly disputes and rework during audits.

rows.stk: Data Integrity / Computer System Validation Specialist
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of validation criteria conflicting with governing regulatory requirements
rows.imp_rat: Validation criteria conflicting with governing regulations creates compliance risk (relevance 3); severity moderate as conflicts can be reconciled.
rows.sat_rat: Validation criteria sometimes conflict with governing regulations because mapping is manual and error-prone. Cross-checking criteria against regulations is a lengthy manual mapping. Aligning validation criteria to evolving regulations requires specialized regulatory knowledge. Misalignment risks costly findings and remediation.

rows.stk: Data Integrity / Computer System Validation Specialist
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable regulatory requirements lacking objective evidence of fulfillment by the record-capture approach
rows.imp_rat: Regulatory requirements lacking objective evidence of fulfillment by the capture approach weakens compliance demonstration (relevance 3); severity moderate and remediable.
rows.sat_rat: Some regulatory requirements lack objective fulfillment evidence for the capture approach, a recurring traceability gap. Assembling objective evidence per requirement is time-intensive. Mapping evidence to each regulatory requirement demands experienced validation staff. Evidence gaps trigger expensive audit remediation.

rows.stk: Data Integrity / Computer System Validation Specialist
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of conflict among data-integrity, validation, and disposition criteria
rows.imp_rat: Conflict among data-integrity/validation/disposition criteria creates ambiguity (relevance 3); severity moderate as conflicts are resolvable before use.
rows.sat_rat: Conflicts among the three criteria sets are common and inconsistently reconciled. Reconciling conflicting criteria takes iterative review time. Detecting and resolving conflicts among integrity, validation and disposition criteria needs cross-domain expertise. Unresolved conflicts cause costly rework and inconsistent decisions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that evaluation criteria overlook partial transfers, duplicate records, unavailable source systems, or reconciliation failures
rows.imp_rat: Criteria overlooking partial transfers/duplicates/source unavailability/reconciliation failures leaves capture gaps (relevance 3); severity moderate as such edge cases can corrupt data if missed.
rows.sat_rat: Evaluation criteria routinely omit these transactional edge cases, so they remain a chronic, largely unserved reliability weakness. Anticipating edge cases like partial transfers and reconciliation failures requires extended failure-mode analysis rarely funded. Foreseeing duplicate records, unavailable sources, and reconciliation failures demands experienced integration engineers who think in failure modes. These overlooked failures cause expensive production data incidents, yet upfront FMEA-style analysis is seldom budgeted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cross-system responsibilities left undefined for identity, access, electronic signatures, audit trails, and record retention
rows.imp_rat: Undefined cross-system responsibilities for identity/signatures/audit/retention create ambiguity (relevance 3); severity moderate as governance can be assigned later.
rows.sat_rat: Responsibility gaps for audit trails and signatures are common at system boundaries and typically discovered only when a control fails. Defining cross-system responsibilities for identity, signatures, and retention is a drawn-out negotiation across departments. Assigning these responsibilities correctly demands combined expertise in Part 11, identity management, and records retention that rarely sits in one place. Undefined responsibilities create duplicated or gapped effort that wastes resources during and after implementation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable data integrity, electronic-record, retention, and inspection-access obligations omitted from evaluation criteria
rows.imp_rat: Omitting data-integrity/retention/inspection obligations from evaluation criteria affects compliance coverage (relevance 3); severity moderate as omissions can be added.
rows.sat_rat: Standard regulatory checklists cover most obligations reliably, though integration-specific inspection-access and retention nuances occasionally slip. Compiling applicable data-integrity and retention obligations adds regulatory-review time but follows known checklists. Regulatory obligations for electronic records are well documented, so competent quality staff can enumerate most requirements without rare expertise. Missing an obligation is costly at inspection, but the compliance frameworks needed are already established assets.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required batch execution data elements whose meaning or context is misaligned across manufacturing and quality systems
rows.imp_rat: Misaligned meaning/context of data elements across systems harms structured integration (relevance 3); severity moderate as mapping issues can be corrected.
rows.sat_rat: Semantic mismatch across MES, LIMS, and QMS is a persistent, poorly mitigated source of error in the status quo. Reconciling data-element meaning across systems is a painstaking, time-intensive semantic mapping effort. Resolving semantic misalignment requires deep domain knowledge of how each system defines and uses each data element. Misaligned meanings cause costly data errors and rework downstream, with minimal upfront mapping investment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of duplicate or conflicting batch execution records created across manufacturing and quality systems
rows.imp_rat: Duplicate/conflicting records across systems is rework that muddies the source of truth (relevance 3); severity moderate as it can be deduplicated in review.
rows.sat_rat: Without strong master-data governance, duplicate and conflicting batch records recur, and prevention is inconsistently designed into integrations. Detecting duplicate or conflicting records across systems requires time-consuming reconciliation processes. Preventing duplication demands understanding of master-data and record-of-truth rules that not all teams have codified. Duplicate and conflicting records generate expensive investigation and correction effort in operations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material access-control, transmission-integrity, or audit-evidence risks left unaddressed by the recommended integration direction
rows.imp_rat: Access-control/transmission/audit risks left unaddressed by the integration direction affect integrity (relevance 3); severity moderate as an architectural risk rather than immediate release error.
rows.sat_rat: Security controls exist as standard IT practice, yet integration-specific transmission and audit-evidence risks are inconsistently addressed in recommendations. Security and audit-evidence risk assessment takes time and often lags the functional evaluation. Access-control and transmission-integrity risk analysis needs infosec expertise that is frequently siloed away from the batch-record team. Unaddressed security risks lead to expensive remediation and audit findings, but preventive security review is often under-resourced.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty that critical manufacturing and quality system dependencies remain undiscovered before the recommendation is finalized
rows.imp_rat: Prolonged uncertainty about undiscovered system dependencies affects recommendation completeness (relevance 3); severity moderate and planning-oriented rather than release-critical.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of approval events lacking signer identity, signing intent, or record linkage
rows.imp_rat: Approval events lacking signer identity/intent/linkage weakens attribution (relevance 3); severity moderate as such gaps can invalidate specific approvals.
rows.sat_rat: Part -compliant e-signatures reliably capture signer identity, intent, and record linkage as standard practice. Electronic signatures capture identity and intent instantly without added delay. Signing workflows are standardized so little specialized skill is needed to ensure completeness. Mature e-signature infrastructure keeps per-event cost low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batch exceptions lacking investigation rationale, impact assessment, or disposition linkage
rows.imp_rat: Exceptions lacking investigation rationale/impact/disposition linkage weaken structured records (relevance 3); severity moderate as linkages can be added in review.
rows.sat_rat: Exception workflows capture most elements but some exceptions still lack full rationale or linkage. Verifying each exception has rationale, impact, and disposition linkage adds review time. Completeness of exception handling depends on trained investigators' diligence. Incomplete exception records trigger rework and findings.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency between captured batch evidence and regulatory submission evidence
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency between captured evidence and regulatory submission evidence affects compliance alignment (relevance 3); severity moderate and reconcilable before submission.
rows.sat_rat: Batch and submission evidence are maintained in separate systems, so inconsistencies persist and are caught unreliably. Reconciling captured batch evidence with submission evidence is a slow cross-checking task. Detecting inconsistencies needs expert familiarity with both the batch record and the submission dossier. Discrepancies surface late and cause expensive submission corrections.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved evidence omissions in batch packages prepared for inspection
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved evidence omissions in inspection packages affect audit readiness (relevance 3); severity moderate since they surface before formal inspection.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-inspection reviews catch many gaps but unresolved omissions still reach inspection-ready packages. Resolving evidence omissions before inspection consumes significant preparation time. Spotting unresolved omissions relies on skilled reviewers' completeness checks. Late discovery during inspection prep creates costly scramble and potential findings.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required reviews and approvals lacking authorized-completion evidence
rows.imp_rat: Reviews/approvals lacking authorized-completion evidence weakens record validity (relevance 3); severity moderate as evidence may be retrievable.
rows.sat_rat: EBR workflows generally enforce authorized-completion evidence, though gaps appear at system boundaries. Workflow-enforced approvals record completion evidence automatically without extra time. Configured review/approval workflows reduce reliance on individual vigilance. Standard workflow tooling makes this inexpensive to maintain.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of deviations and exceptions lacking linked supporting evidence during compliance review
rows.imp_rat: Deviations/exceptions lacking linked supporting evidence during compliance review weaken records (relevance 3); severity moderate as evidence can be located during review.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation management systems provide some linkage but evidence still commonly goes unlinked at review time. Linking deviations to supporting evidence during review takes extra manual effort. Reviewers must judge which evidence supports each deviation, a skill-dependent linkage. Unlinked deviations cause rework and inspection questions that cost time and credibility.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of translated requirements that fail to specify the batch evidence needed for compliance evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Translated requirements failing to specify needed batch evidence introduce ambiguity into what to capture (relevance 3); severity moderate as it degrades but does not halt evaluation.
rows.sat_rat: Many translated requirements remain vague about the specific evidence needed, so compliance evaluation is inconsistently supported. Specifying the batch evidence needed for each requirement is slow manual translation work. It requires expert judgement to convert a regulatory clause into the concrete evidence that demonstrates compliance. The dependence on skilled translators and later corrections raises cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of translated batch-evaluation requirements lacking objective acceptance conditions
rows.imp_rat: Translated batch-evaluation requirements lacking objective acceptance conditions create evaluation ambiguity (relevance 3); severity moderate since criteria can be clarified downstream.
rows.sat_rat: A large share of translated requirements still rely on subjective judgement rather than objective acceptance conditions, producing inconsistent conclusions. Adding objective acceptance conditions to each requirement is a painstaking manual effort. Defining objective, testable acceptance conditions demands scarce expertise to avoid ambiguity. Expert authoring time and downstream disputes over subjective criteria drive cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts among regulatory sources applicable to batch evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflicts among applicable regulatory sources creates evaluation ambiguity (relevance 3); severity moderate as conflicts are resolvable before decision.
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory conflicts are largely handled ad hoc, so unresolved conflicts routinely persist unnoticed. Identifying and resolving conflicts among regulatory sources is a lengthy expert-driven analysis. Reconciling conflicting regulatory requirements demands rare, senior interpretive expertise. Conflict resolution consumes costly expert time and often external counsel.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batch data changes lacking attributable identity or documented rationale
rows.imp_rat: Batch data changes lacking attributable identity or rationale is a data-integrity gap (relevance 3); severity moderate as it undermines but does not always void the record.
rows.sat_rat: Part trails reliably attribute identity, though documented rationale is sometimes weak or perfunctory. Audit trails attribute changes and prompt for rationale in real time. Change-control prompts reduce dependence on operator memory or expertise. Built-in audit trail functionality carries low incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between processing-step execution and completion confirmation
rows.imp_rat: Delay between step execution and completion confirmation affects timeliness (relevance 3); severity low as it is a lag rather than a data corruption.
rows.sat_rat: Confirmation eventually happens reliably but not always contemporaneously. The gap between executing a step and confirming completion can be notable when sign-off is batched. Timely confirmation needs operator discipline. Delayed confirmation slows downstream steps and disposition.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the completion criteria for each processing step
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in step completion criteria affects capture clarity (relevance 3); severity low as it mainly causes minor confusion rather than release harm.
rows.sat_rat: Well-written SOPs reduce ambiguity but interpretation gaps persist. Ambiguous completion criteria cause hesitation and clarification delays. Interpreting fuzzy completion criteria falls on operator judgement, the core burden here. Ambiguity yields inconsistent completion and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that a critical process parameter remains outside its approved range during the run
rows.imp_rat: Duration a parameter stays outside its approved range is an in-run stability concern peripheral to capture/structuring (relevance 2); severity moderate given quality impact.
rows.sat_rat: Without continuous control, excursion duration is inconsistently managed. Reducing time a CPP stays out of range depends on how fast excursions are detected and corrected. Rapid correction needs skilled operator response. Prolonged excursion risks scrapping product, a major cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration between each batch execution event and its corresponding data entry
rows.imp_rat: Delay between each execution event and its data entry affects contemporaneous capture (relevance 3); severity low as short lags rarely corrupt the record.
rows.sat_rat: Real-time capture makes long event-to-entry gaps uncommon in EBR environments. Contemporaneous electronic entry at point of execution nearly eliminates the delay between event and recording, directly serving this need. Point-of-use entry reduces reliance on memory and later transcription skill. Immediate entry avoids costs of reconstructing data later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batch-record entries requiring reconciliation because recorded values conflict
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting recorded values requiring reconciliation is rework affecting data quality (relevance 3); severity low since it is caught and corrected in review.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic single-entry capture reduces value conflicts substantially, though multi-source entries still generate some. Reconciliation of conflicting values consumes review time when triggered. Reconciling conflicting recorded values requires attentive expert review. Reconciliation work adds labor cost per affected batch.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of EBR data fields requiring reconciliation because captured values conflict with approved source records
rows.imp_rat: EBR fields requiring reconciliation because they conflict with approved source records is rework (relevance 3); severity low as it is caught and corrected before disposition.
rows.sat_rat: Automated interfaces reduce conflicts where systems are integrated, but manual entry points still generate them. Reconciling conflicting fields is a time drain but the error is the conflict itself. Reconciliation is tedious more than skill-intensive when source records are clear. Field reconciliation consumes reviewer labor repeatedly.

rows.stk: Data Integrity / Computer System Validation Specialist
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of validation criteria that cannot be assessed from obtainable objective evidence
rows.imp_rat: Validation criteria that cannot be assessed from obtainable evidence weakens verifiability (relevance 3); severity low as it is a specification refinement issue.
rows.sat_rat: A share of validation criteria remain unassessable from available evidence, undermining conclusions. Determining assessability of criteria adds planning time. Judging whether obtainable evidence can support a criterion needs expert framing. Unassessable criteria waste validation effort and money.

rows.stk: Data Integrity / Computer System Validation Specialist
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in acceptance criteria for data-integrity requirements
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in acceptance criteria for data-integrity requirements affects clarity (relevance 3); severity low as it is clarifiable specification work.
rows.sat_rat: Data-integrity acceptance criteria often remain ambiguous, causing inconsistent pass/fail judgements. Sharpening acceptance criteria is upfront specification work. Defining unambiguous data-integrity acceptance criteria requires expert authorship. Vague criteria drive expensive rework and re-testing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of required batch review and disposition use cases unsupported by the recommended integration direction
rows.imp_rat: Review/disposition use cases unsupported by the integration direction is a coverage gap (relevance 3); severity low as it is a design shortfall correctable in planning.
rows.sat_rat: Common review and disposition use cases are usually covered, though less-frequent scenarios are sometimes left unsupported. Mapping use-case coverage takes time but leverages known review and disposition workflows. Enumerating review and disposition use cases draws on familiar quality operations knowledge that is reasonably available. Unsupported use cases force costly manual workarounds later, but the core use cases are generally understood upfront.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batch actions lacking links to applicable regulatory provisions
rows.imp_rat: Batch actions lacking links to regulatory provisions affects traceability completeness (relevance 3); severity low as it is largely a documentation-linkage gap.
rows.sat_rat: Batch actions are seldom explicitly linked to regulatory provisions, so this linkage is largely missing today. Linking each batch action to applicable regulatory provisions is slow manual traceability work. It requires deep regulatory knowledge to map actions to provisions, rarely automated. The manual traceability effort is labor-intensive and error correction is costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of deviation indications raised while actual conditions remain acceptable
rows.imp_rat: False deviation indications while conditions are acceptable are mostly nuisance rework (relevance 2, severity 2), peripheral to correct capture and low consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Threshold tuning is imperfect, so nuisance indications recur. False deviation alerts waste time in unnecessary assessment. Judging a false alarm needs operator experience. False positives cost investigation effort and lost throughput.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of critical process parameter fluctuation near approved boundaries during the run
rows.imp_rat: Parameter fluctuation near approved boundaries is an in-run stability concern remote from capture/structuring (relevance 2); severity low unless a limit is actually breached.
rows.sat_rat: Automated control loops handle this well where present; manual control is less consistent. Managing near-boundary fluctuation is a continuous real-time task. Holding a parameter steady near limits requires skilled process control. Boundary fluctuation risks out-of-spec product cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between the recorded batch state and physical batch state after interruption
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between recorded and physical batch state after interruption threatens data fidelity, yet ranked lower; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Post-interruption reconciliation is manual and error-prone, a recurring gap. Reconciling recorded and physical state after interruption takes time. Accurate reconciliation demands operator understanding of what happened. State mismatch can cause wrong resumption and batch loss cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a required supporting attachment is inaccessible during batch record review
rows.imp_rat: Inaccessible supporting attachments during review harms record completeness, but ranked lower; capped.
rows.sat_rat: EBRs link many attachments reliably, but externally stored or unlinked documents still occasionally can't be found during review. Electronic attachment linking makes most supporting attachments instantly accessible at review, though missing ones stall it. Direct links reduce reliance on reviewers knowing where attachments live. Inaccessible attachments cause costly review delays.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required execution steps lacking completion evidence during conformance assessment
rows.imp_rat: Required steps lacking completion evidence affects capture completeness, but ranked lower; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic step tracking flags many missing completions, but manually executed or out-of-system steps still lack evidence at a real rate. Completion-evidence checks during conformance assessment are faster than paper reconciliation. Step-completion tracking reduces reliance on reviewer memory of required steps. Missing completion evidence forces costly review holds.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of aseptic interventions lacking required details for procedural conformance assessment
rows.imp_rat: Aseptic interventions lacking required detail weakens captured evidence, but ranked lower; both proxies held.
rows.sat_rat: Aseptic-intervention detail is often captured inconsistently and outside structured fields, so required details for conformance assessment are frequently incomplete. Assembling required intervention details for conformance assessment is time-consuming. Knowing the required detail set for procedural conformance requires trained aseptic reviewers. Insufficient intervention detail forces costly rework or reprocessing decisions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved deficiencies marked as corrected in batch record review status
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved deficiencies marked corrected corrupts review status, but ranked below batch 2; both proxies held.
rows.sat_rat: Status flags can be set without verified resolution, so unresolved deficiencies are sometimes marked corrected. Verifying that a deficiency marked corrected is actually resolved takes reviewer time. Confirming true resolution demands attentive reviewers, not just status flags. Falsely closed deficiencies risk releasing defective batches.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batch events attributed to an incorrect operator, material lot, component lot, or equipment identity
rows.imp_rat: Events attributed to wrong operator/lot/equipment corrupts captured attribution, yet ranking caps it; held.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode/ID capture makes identity misattribution fairly uncommon, though manual overrides still introduce some errors. Scanned operator, lot and equipment IDs speed accurate attribution. ID scanning reduces reliance on manual transcription of identities. Misattributed identities cause costly traceability investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of entries belonging to another batch included in the record under review
rows.imp_rat: Entries from another batch in the record threatens data integrity, but ranked lower; both proxies held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-scoped electronic records make inclusion of another batch's entries rare where the system enforces context. Batch-scoped records prevent reviewers from sifting foreign entries. Automatic batch binding removes reliance on reviewer vigilance to exclude other batches. Excluding foreign entries avoids costly misdisposition risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of aseptic interventions or line stoppages lacking required execution evidence
rows.imp_rat: Interventions/stoppages lacking execution evidence undermines the record, but sits below batch 2; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Interventions and line stoppages often lack structured evidence capture, so required execution evidence is regularly missing. Gathering execution evidence for interventions and line stoppages is slow manual work. Determining what evidence each intervention requires depends on expert reviewers. Missing intervention evidence risks costly sterility-assurance holds.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required supporting records unavailable or unmatched to the batch
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable/unmatched supporting records weakens the structured record, but sits below batch 2; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Supporting records from LIMS, QMS and paper sources are often not auto-matched, so some remain unavailable or unmatched at review. Locating and matching required supporting records to the batch is slow when they live in other systems. Matching disparate records to the batch requires familiarity with multiple systems. Unmatched records force costly disposition delays.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of captured batch execution actions not traceable to a responsible individual
rows.imp_rat: Untraceable actions weaken attributability for capture/structure, but the ranking caps this below batch 2; relevance to structuring data is real yet secondary, severity moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Enforced electronic signatures and user authentication make untraceable actions almost nonexistent, among the best-served needs. Automatic user logging removes any manual traceability effort. Login-based attribution eliminates dependence on user diligence to record who acted. Reliable attribution avoids costly integrity remediation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unauthorized access events or data changes affecting disposition-relevant batch records
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized access/changes threaten record integrity, but this batch sits below prior items; relevance and severity held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Validated access controls and audit trails make unauthorized access/change events affecting records rare and detectable. Access controls operate automatically with no added review time. Role-based security reduces reliance on manual vigilance against unauthorized changes. Preventing unauthorized changes avoids expensive compliance failures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of overdue batch-affecting deviations not escalated before disposition
rows.imp_rat: Overdue deviations unescalated before disposition affects completeness of review inputs, but ranked lower; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Overdue deviations sometimes reach disposition unescalated because tracking-to-escalation linkage is weak. Escalating overdue deviations before disposition is a timing-driven need often missed under load. Escalation is procedural rather than expertise-intensive. Unescalated overdue deviations delay release and tie up capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required reviews or approvals incomplete when the disposition decision is due
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete reviews/approvals at decision time delay disposition, but the item ranks below batch 2; both proxies at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Workflow routing improves completion tracking, but approval bottlenecks still leave reviews incomplete at decision time. Incomplete reviews/approvals at decision time are a common source of last-minute delay. Completing reviews depends on availability of qualified approvers rather than special skill. Waiting on approvals ties up batches, adding carrying cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that evidence from another batch or product is included in disposition
rows.imp_rat: Cross-batch evidence contamination in disposition harms record integrity, but ranked below batch 2; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-keyed records and identifiers make cross-batch evidence inclusion uncommon. Verifying batch identity is quick, so time is minor.
